---
title: "Appendix S1"
subtitle: "A comparison of methods for estimating the health effects of exposures over the life course: adiposity and systemic inflammation in the 1958 National Child Development Study"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-location: body
    toc-depth: 2
    toc-expand: 3
    number-sections: true
    code-fold: true
    code-overflow: wrap
    code-summary: "Show the code"
    embed-resources: true
    crossrefs-hover: true
editor: source
execute:
  echo: true
  warning: false
  message: false
knitr:
  opts_chunk:
    dev: "png"
bibliography: references.bib
csl: american-medical-association.csl
link-citations: true
fig-cap-location: top
crossref:
  fig-title: "Figure S"
  tbl-title: "Table S"
  fig-prefix: "Figure S"
  tbl-prefix: "Table S"
---

```{=html}
<style>
/* ── Page-like layout ─────────────────────────────────────── */
body {
  font-family: "Palatino Linotype", Palatino, "Book Antiqua", Georgia, serif;
  font-size: 9.6pt;
  line-height: 1.4;
  color: #1a1a1a;
  background-color: #f8f8f8;
}

main.content {
  max-width: 62em;
  margin-left: auto;
  margin-right: auto;
  background-color: #ffffff;
  padding: 2.5em 3em;
  box-shadow: 0 0 12px rgba(0, 0, 0, 0.08);
  border: 1px solid #e0e0e0;
}

/* ── Title block ──────────────────────────────────────────── */
.quarto-title h1.title {
  font-size: 1.6em;
  font-weight: 700;
  line-height: 1.3;
  margin-bottom: 0.2em;
}

.quarto-title .subtitle {
  font-size: 1.2em;
  font-weight: 400;
  color: #555;
}

/* ── Headings ─────────────────────────────────────────────── */
h1, h2, h3, h4 {
  font-family: "Palatino Linotype", Palatino, "Book Antiqua", Georgia, serif;
  color: #1a1a1a;
  margin-top: 1.8em;
  margin-bottom: 0.6em;
}

h1 { font-size: 1.45em; border-bottom: 1.5px solid #cccccc; padding-bottom: 0.25em; }
h2 { font-size: 1.2em; }
h3 { font-size: 1.05em; font-style: italic; }

/* ── Paragraph spacing ────────────────────────────────────── */
p { margin-bottom: 0.9em; text-align: justify; }

/* ── Figure and table captions ────────────────────────────── */
.quarto-figure-center > figure > p,
.quarto-figure-center > figure > div {
  text-align: left !important;
}

.quarto-figure-center > figure > figcaption,
figcaption,
.table-caption,
caption {
  text-align: left !important;
  font-size: 0.95em;
  color: #333;
  font-style: italic;
  margin-top: 0.4em;
  margin-bottom: 0.8em;
}

/* ── Tables ───────────────────────────────────────────────── */
.quarto-figure-center > figure > div table {
  font-size: 0.85em;
}

table {
  border-collapse: collapse;
  width: 100%;
  font-size: 0.85em;
  margin-bottom: 1.2em;
}

table th {
  border-bottom: 2px solid #333;
  padding: 0.4em 0.6em;
  text-align: left;
  font-weight: 600;
}

table td {
  border-bottom: 1px solid #ddd;
  padding: 0.35em 0.6em;
}

table tbody tr:last-child td {
  border-bottom: 2px solid #333;
}

/* ── GT / gtsummary tables ───────────────────────────────── */
div#quarto-document-content .gt_table,
.gtsummary,
div.cell-output-display table.gt_table {
  font-size: 0.8em !important;
}

div#quarto-document-content .gt_table th,
div#quarto-document-content .gt_table td,
.gt_table .gt_row,
.gt_table .gt_col_heading {
  padding: 2px 5px !important;
}

/* ── Code blocks ──────────────────────────────────────────── */
pre.sourceCode {
  background-color: #f5f5f5;
  border: 1px solid #e0e0e0;
  border-radius: 3px;
  font-size: 0.82em;
  padding: 0.8em 1em;
}

code { font-size: 0.88em; }

div.sourceCode { margin-bottom: 1em; }

/* ── TOC styling ──────────────────────────────────────────── */
#TOC {
  font-size: 0.95em;
  border-left: 2px solid #ccc;
  padding-left: 1em;
  margin-bottom: 2em;
}

/* ── Horizontal rules (section dividers) ──────────────────── */
hr {
  border: none;
  border-top: 1.5px solid #aaa;
  margin: 2.5em 0;
}

/* ── Lists ────────────────────────────────────────────────── */
ul, ol { margin-bottom: 0.9em; }
li { margin-bottom: 0.3em; }
</style>
```

This appendix provides full technical details for the seven life course methods compared in the main paper, using BMI and systemic inflammation (hsCRP) in the 1958 National Child Development Study (NCDS) as a running empirical example. For each method, we give a brief description, provide implementation choices, and results, with supporting figures and tables. All analyses were conducted in R; code is embedded throughout and can be revealed using the "Show the code" toggle.


```{r}
library(here)
library(gtsummary)
library(gt)
library(mgcv) 
library(tidygam)
library(lcmm)
library(marginaleffects)
source(here("Code", "Clean_NCDS.R"))
source(here("Code", "IOTF_BMI.R"))
set_gtsummary_theme(theme_gtsummary_compact(set_theme = TRUE))
```

```{r}
# Load the fitted Hierarchical GAM model for BMI predictions
mod.BMI.fs.bam <- readRDS(here("Data", "Models", "mod.BMI.fs.bam.rds"))

# Create predictions from Hierarchical GAM model
df.bmi_prediction <- expand.grid(
  xage = 7:56,
  self_report = 0,
  ncdsid = unique(df.NCDS.HBMI.complete.analytic$ncdsid)) %>%
  left_join(  
    df.NCDS.HBMI.complete.analytic %>%
    select(ncdsid, sex) %>%
    filter(!is.na(sex)) %>%
    distinct() %>%
    group_by(ncdsid) %>%
    summarise(sex = first(sex), .groups = "drop"),
  by = "ncdsid"
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    pred_bmi = predict(mod.BMI.fs.bam, newdata = ., type = "response")
  ) %>%
  mutate(sex = ifelse(sex == "Female", "F", "M")) %>%
  left_join(iotf_cutoffs, by = c("sex", "xage" = "age")) %>% 
  mutate(
    bmi_category = case_when(
      pred_bmi < 25   ~ "Normal",
      xage >= 18 & pred_bmi < 30   ~ "Overweight",
      xage >= 18                   ~ "Obese",
      xage < 18 & pred_bmi < overweight  ~ "Normal",
      xage < 18 & pred_bmi < obese       ~ "Overweight",
      xage < 18                          ~ "Obese",
      TRUE ~ NA_character_
    )
  )

```


# Analytic Sample

This section describes the derivation of the analytic sample from the NCDS Wave 10 interview cohort and provides descriptive statistics for all variables used in subsequent analyses. @fig-s1 presents the sample flow diagram, detailing each exclusion criterion applied from initial Wave 10 participation through nurse visit consent, blood sample collection, valid hsCRP measurement, removal of values above 10 mg/L (indicating possible acute infection), completeness of early life covariates, and the requirement of at least three longitudinal BMI measurements. @tbl-s1 summarizes the distributions of the outcome (log-transformed hsCRP), BMI averaged across three life periods, and all covariates in the final analytic sample. @tbl-s2 reports the distribution of the number of BMI observations per respondent, reflecting the density of the longitudinal BMI data available for exposure modeling.

```{r}
#| label: fig-s1
#| fig-height: 8
#| fig-width: 5
#| out-width: "75%"
#| fig-cap: "Sample flow diagram showing the derivation of the analytic sample from the NCDS Wave 10 cohort."

library(DiagrammeR)

grViz("
  digraph sample_flow {
    
    # Graph settings
    graph [rankdir = TB, fontname = 'Helvetica', nodesep = 0.3, ranksep = 0.4]
    node [shape = box, fontname = 'Helvetica', fontsize = 10, width = 3.5]
    edge [fontname = 'Helvetica', fontsize = 9]
    
    # Main flow nodes
    n0 [label = 'Wave 10 Interviews\\nn = 8,405']
    n1 [label = 'Agreed to nurse visit contact\\nn = 7,166\\n(1,239 not asked/declined)']
    n2 [label = 'Participated in nurse visit\\nn = 6,309\\n(857 did not participate)']
    n3 [label = 'Blood samples analyzed\\nn = 5,117\\n(1,192 no consent/ineligible)']
    n4 [label = 'Valid hsCRP values\\nn = 4,179\\n(938 invalid/missing)']
    n5 [label = 'hsCRP < 10 mg/L\\nn = 4,014\\n(165 outliers removed)']
    n6 [label = 'Complete early life covariates\\nn = 2,255\\n(1,759 missing covariates)']
    n7 [label = 'Final analytic sample\\n≥3 BMI measures\\nn = 2,197\\n(58 excluded)', style = 'filled', fillcolor = 'lightblue']

    # Main flow edges
    n0 -> n1
    n1 -> n2
    n2 -> n3
    n3 -> n4
    n4 -> n5
    n5 -> n6
    n6 -> n7
  }
  ")
```

```{r}
#| tbl-cap: "Descriptive statistics for the analytic sample."
#| label: tbl-s1
df.bmi_prediction %>% 
  group_by(ncdsid) %>%
  select(ncdsid, xage, pred_bmi) %>%
  summarise(
    mean_bmi_early_life = mean(pred_bmi[xage >= 7 & xage <= 25], na.rm = TRUE), # early life BMI (7-25 years)
    mean_bmi_early_midlife = mean(pred_bmi[xage > 25 & xage <= 40], na.rm = TRUE), # early midlife BMI (26-40 years)
    mean_bmi_late_midlife = mean(pred_bmi[xage > 40 & xage <= 60], na.rm = TRUE) # late midlife BMI (41-60 years)
  ) %>% 
  right_join(df.model, by = "ncdsid") %>%
  select(loghsCRP, starts_with("mean_bmi"), all_of(covs)) %>%
  filter(complete.cases(.)) %>% 
  tbl_summary(
    include = c(loghsCRP, sweep10_age, sex, mean_bmi_early_life, mean_bmi_early_midlife, mean_bmi_late_midlife, educ_qual, father_bmi, mother_bmi, n646, n607, n2383),
    statistic = list(
      all_continuous() ~ "{mean} ({sd})",
      all_categorical() ~ "{n} ({p}%)"
    ),
    label = list(
      loghsCRP ~ "log(hsCRP)",
      mean_bmi_early_life ~ "Mean BMI (7-25 years)",
      mean_bmi_early_midlife ~ "Mean BMI (26-40 years)",
      mean_bmi_late_midlife ~ "Mean BMI (41-56 years)",
      sex ~ "Sex",
      sweep10_age ~ "Age at hsCRP measurement (years)",
      educ_qual ~ "Educational qualification",
      father_bmi ~ "Father's BMI",
      mother_bmi ~ "Mother's BMI",
      n646 ~ "Birthweight",
      n607 ~ "Person's per room",
      n2383 ~ "Father's occupation"
    ),
    missing = "ifany",
    missing_text = "Missing"
  ) %>%
  italicize_levels() %>%
  modify_table_body(
    ~ .x %>%
      add_row(
        .before = which(.x$variable == "educ_qual"),
        label = "     Early Childhood Covariates",
        variable = NA,
        stat_0 = ""
      )
  )
```

```{r}                                                                 
#| tbl-cap: "Distribution of the number of longitudinal BMI observations available per respondent in the analytic sample."
#| label: tbl-s2

# Analyze the distribution of BMI measurements per respondent
df.NCDS.HBMI.complete.analytic %>%
    group_by(ncdsid) %>%
    summarise(n_visits = n()) %>%
    count(n_visits) %>%
    rename(`Number of BMI Observations` = n_visits,
           `Number of Respondents` = n) %>%
    mutate(`Percent` = round(100 * `Number of Respondents` / sum(`Number of Respondents`), 1)) %>%
    gt() %>%
    fmt_number(columns = `Number of Respondents`, decimals = 0) %>%
    cols_align(align = "center")
```


BMI measures were drawn from a consolidated dataset (CLOSER Work Package 1) including harmonized height, weight, and BMI measures from the five British longitudinal cohort studies including the NCDS.[@closer2019] Childhood and adolescent BMI values (ages 7, 11, and 16) were derived from direct physical measurements taken by a nurse. Adult BMI values were obtained from a mix of self-reported and measured data, depending on wave: weight and height at ages 23, 42, and 50 were self-reported, while assessments at ages 33 and 44 were completed by trained interviewers or nurses. BMI at age 55 was derived from self-reported height and weight collected via web or telephone interview as part of the age 55 wave.[@cls2024]

# Modeling BMI Exposure

The hierarchical GAM was implemented using the computationally efficient `bam()` function in the `mgcv` package in R.[@wood2025; @wood2015] Modeling BMI as a smooth trajectory is appropriate because the analysis targets longer-term patterns of adiposity rather than short-term fluctuations in weight between measurement occasions. Smoothing reduces noise due to irregular timing while preserving the broader exposure patterns. To ensure appropriate flexibility in exposure patterns, we estimated a separate non-linear BMI trajectory for each individual while constraining trajectories to share the same degree of smoothness (Model S structure) [@pedersen2019] (see Figure 1 in main text). The model also includes a fixed effect for measurement mode (directly measured vs. self-reported) to account for systematic reporting differences. This approach to imputation reduces noise due to irregular measurement timing, accounts for systematic reporting differences, and provides consistent longitudinal BMI trajectories for each respondent. Statistical code for model fitting is in "Clean_NCDS.R" file.

:::: {#fig-s2-bmi layout-ncol=2 fig-cap="Predicted BMI trajectories from the hierarchical GAM. A) Population mean trajectory with 95% confidence interval. B) Individual predicted trajectories for a random sample of 16 respondents, with observed BMI values shown by measurement type."}

```{r}
#| label: fig-s2-bmi-a
#| fig-cap: "Population-Level BMI Trajectory"
#| fig.dpi: 60

# Create population-level prediction data by aggregating individual predictions
population_data <- df.bmi_prediction %>%
  group_by(xage) %>%
  summarise(
    pred_bmi = mean(pred_bmi, na.rm = TRUE),
    se_fit = sd(pred_bmi, na.rm = TRUE) / sqrt(n()),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    lower_ci = pred_bmi - 1.96 * se_fit,
    upper_ci = pred_bmi + 1.96 * se_fit
  )

# Plot population-level BMI trajectory
population_data %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = xage, y = pred_bmi)) +
  geom_ribbon(aes(ymin = lower_ci, ymax = upper_ci), alpha = 0.6, fill = "lightgray") +
  geom_line(color = "steelblue", size = 1.2) +
  labs(x = "Age (years)", 
       y = "Predicted BMI", 
       title = "Population Mean BMI Trajectory from GAM") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 14))
```

```{r}
#| label: fig-s2-bmi-b
#| fig-cap: "Individual BMI Trajectories (Sample of 16)"
#| fig.dpi: 60

set.seed(1234)  # For reproducibility

df.sampleplot <-
  df.NCDS.HBMI.complete.analytic %>% 
  group_by(ncdsid) %>%
  nest() %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  slice_sample(n = 16) %>%  # Sample 25 random observations
  unnest() 
  
# Plot the sampled 20 individuals
df.sampleplot %>%
  mutate(measurement_type = ifelse(self_report == 1, "Self-reported", "Measured")) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = xage, y = bmi)) +
    # Scatter plot with measurement type in the aesthetic
    geom_point(aes(color = measurement_type), alpha = 0.6, size = 1.2) +
    # GAM model predictions for the same 16 individuals
    geom_line(data = df.bmi_prediction %>% 
                filter(ncdsid %in% unique(df.sampleplot$ncdsid)), 
              aes(x = xage, y = pred_bmi, color = "Individual Predictions"), 
              size = 0.8) +
    facet_wrap(~ncdsid) +  
    # Labels and Theme
    labs(x = "Age (years)", y = "BMI", color = "Data Type", title = "Predicted BMI Trajectories for Sample of Respondents") +
    scale_color_manual(values = c(
      "Measured" = "black",
      "Self-reported" = "darkred",
      "Individual Predictions" = "steelblue")) +
    theme_minimal() +
    theme(strip.text = element_blank(),  # Remove facet titles
          legend.position = "bottom",
          plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 14))  # Move legend to the bottom
```

::::

# Lifecourse Methods Summary Table {.smaller .custom-small}

@tbl-s16-summary provides a side-by-side comparison of all seven life course methods, summarizing the key estimand produced by each approach, the primary research question it addresses, and an example substantive conclusion drawn from the BMI--hsCRP analysis presented in this appendix.

| Method | Key Estimands | Primary Research Questions | Example Substantive Conclusion from BMI-hsCRP Analysis |
|--------|---------------|---------------------------|-------------------------------------------------------|
| **Single Summary** | Average increase in log(hsCRP) associated with a 1-unit increase in mean BMI across ages 7–56, conditional on early-life covariates | Does overall/cumulative BMI exposure across the life course predict inflammation in mid-to-late life? | Each 1-unit increase in mean BMI across ages 7-56 is associated with 0.09-unit higher log(hsCRP) (95% CI: 0.08, 0.11, p<0.001) |
| **Time Period Comparison** | Period-specific associations between BMI and log(hsCRP), estimated either as marginal associations (separate models) or conditional on exposure in other periods (conditional models) | Which life stage(s) of BMI exposure are associated with inflammation? Does BMI during one period matter more than others? | All three life periods show significant associations with log(hsCRP): Early life (ages 7-25: β=0.06), early midlife (ages 26-40: β=0.07) and late midlife (ages 41-57: β=0.07)|
| **Multiple Informant** | (1) Period-specific marginal associations between BMI and log(hsCRP) for each life stage, and (2) contrasts in these associations across periods, represented by BMI-by-period interaction terms | Are the associations between BMI and hsCRP statistically different across life stages? Is there evidence for a critical period? | All three periods show significant positive associations (β=0.07).|
| **Sequence Analysis** | The average difference in log(hsCRP) between individuals belonging to distinct BMI sequence clusters | What distinct patterns of BMI state transitions exist across the life course? Do these patterns predict different inflammation levels? | Compared to the "Normal" (stable normal) reference group, the "Normal-Overweight" sequence shows 0.45-unit higher log(hsCRP), and the "Overweight-Obese" sequence shows 0.79-unit higher log(hsCRP) |
| **Latent Class Trajectory Modeling** | The average difference in log(hsCRP) between individuals belonging to different latent BMI trajectory classes | What are the most common BMI trajectory patterns? Do individuals following different BMI trajectories have different levels of inflammation? | Compared to "Normal BMI" reference class, "Slightly elevated BMI" shows no significant difference in hsCRP, while "High BMI"  and "High BMI and Increasing" classes show 0.35 and 0.475 higher log(hsCRP) respectively|
| **Distributed Lag Non-linear Models** | (1) Lag-specific associations between BMI at a given age and log(hsCRP) for specified BMI contrasts relative to a centering value; (2) Cumulative effects aggregating exposure contributions across the exposure window | How does the effect of BMI on inflammation vary across exposure timing (age) and intensity (BMI level)? What is the cumulative effect of sustaining a certain BMI across the life course? | Using BMI=25 as reference, the cumulative effect of maintaining BMI=20 across ages 7-56 is -0.15-unit lower log(hsCRP), while maintaining BMI=30 results in 0.03-unit higher log(hsCRP) |
| **Structured Life Course Modeling Approach** | The association between log(hsCRP) and the exposure representation corresponding to the selected life course hypothesis (e.g., sensitive period or mobility/change variables) | Which theoretical life course mechanism best explains how BMI affects inflammation? Is it level, change, or duration above threshold? | BMI change early midlife to late midlife shows the strongest association (β=0.117) controlling for mean BMI in life stages|

: Life Course Methods: Estimands, Research Questions, and Example Results {#tbl-s16-summary tbl-colwidths="[12,33,25,30]"}

# Single Numeric Summaries

The single numeric summary approach reduces longitudinal exposure histories to a single scalar quantity intended to summarize exposure burden over a specified period. Depending on how the summary is defined (e.g., mean exposure, cumulative exposure, time above a threshold), this framework may reflect an accumulation-of-risk hypothesis (e.g., mean BMI) or a sensitive period hypothesis (if restricted to a specific window) in which exposure during that window is assumed to have specific influence over later-life outcomes. Here we use the mean BMI across ages 7-56 and include it as an exposure variable in a linear regression predicting log(hsCRP). This is the simplest life course method, treating all time points as equally informative and assuming that a single aggregate captures the relevant exposure signal. @tbl-s3-single presents the fully adjusted regression results, including the effect of mean lifetime BMI on log(hsCRP) alongside all covariates.

```{r}

df.singlesummary <- df.bmi_prediction %>% 
  group_by(ncdsid) %>%
  select(ncdsid, pred_bmi, bmi_category) %>%
  summarise(
    mean_bmi = mean(pred_bmi, na.rm = TRUE),
    yrs_normal      = sum(bmi_category == "Normal", na.rm = TRUE),
    yrs_overweight  = sum(bmi_category == "Overweight", na.rm = TRUE),
    yrs_obese       = sum(bmi_category == "Obese", na.rm = TRUE)
  ) %>% 
  right_join(df.model, by = "ncdsid")


bmi_var <- "mean_bmi"
outcomes <- c("loghsCRP")

# Run single summary model
model.single <- map(outcomes, function(outcome) {
  all_covs <- c(bmi_var, covs)
  formula <- reformulate(all_covs, response = outcome)

  df.singlesummary %>%
    select(all_of(c(outcome, all_covs))) %>%
    lm(formula, data = .)
})

saveRDS(model.single, file = here("Data", "Models", "model.single.rds"))

```

```{r}
#| label: tbl-s3-single
#| tbl-cap: "Adjusted linear regression of log(hsCRP) on mean lifetime BMI (ages 7--56), treating the entire trajectory as a single numeric summary."
model.single %>%
  .[[1]] %>%
  tbl_regression(
    label = list(
      mean_bmi ~ "Mean BMI",
      sex ~ "Sex",
      sweep10_age ~ "Age at hsCRP measurement (years)",
      educ_qual ~ "Educational qualification",
      father_bmi ~ "Father's BMI",
      mother_bmi ~ "Mother's BMI",
      n646 ~ "Birthweight",
      n607 ~ "Person's per room",
      n2383 ~ "Father's occupation"
    )
  ) %>%
  add_significance_stars(hide_ci = FALSE) %>%
  bold_p() %>%
  bold_labels()
```

<hr style="border: 2px solid black;">

# Time Period Comparisons

Rather than collapsing exposure across the entire life span or a subset thereof, this approach partitions the life course into discrete windows intended to correspond to theoretically meaningful stages (e.g., childhood, adolescence, adulthood). Exposure within each period is aggregated using a summary statistic, and these period-specific summaries are included as predictors in regression models in separate models or included simultaneously in a single model. Conditional models estimate associations in each period conditional on exposure in other periods, allowing comparisons of relative importance across life stages. Because the conditioning set determines the contrast being estimated, interpretation depends on which life stages are included in the model.

Here the life course is divided into early life (ages 7--25), early midlife (ages 26--40), and late midlife (ages 41--56), and BMI exposure within each period is examined as a predictor of log(hsCRP). Two modeling strategies are presented. First, separate models fit one period-specific BMI measure at a time, allowing each period's unconditional association with inflammation to be estimated independently (@tbl-s5a-mean through @tbl-s5d-yrs-latemid). Both continuous (mean BMI) and categorical (years spent in each BMI category) summaries are examined for each period. Second, conditional models enter BMI from multiple periods together, estimating each period's contribution after adjusting for BMI at other life stages (@tbl-s4-conditional). The conditional approach uses a stepwise inclusion strategy with a first model that includes early life alone, then early life plus early midlife, then all three periods to assess how effect estimates attenuate as additional periods are conditioned upon.

## Separate Models by Time Period

```{r}
df.timeperiod <- df.bmi_prediction %>%
    group_by(ncdsid) %>%
    summarise(
      # Mean BMI by period
      mean_bmi_early_life = mean(pred_bmi[xage >= 7 & xage <= 25], na.rm = TRUE),
      mean_bmi_early_midlife = mean(pred_bmi[xage > 25 & xage <= 40], na.rm = TRUE),
      mean_bmi_late_midlife = mean(pred_bmi[xage > 40 & xage <= 60], na.rm = TRUE),

      # Years in each BMI category - Early life (7-25)
      yrs_normal_earlylife = sum(bmi_category == "Normal" & xage >= 7 & xage <= 25, na.rm = TRUE),
      yrs_overweight_earlylife = sum(bmi_category == "Overweight" & xage >= 7 & xage <= 25, na.rm = TRUE),
      yrs_obese_earlylife = sum(bmi_category == "Obese" & xage >= 7 & xage <= 25, na.rm = TRUE),

      # Years in each BMI category - Early midlife (25-40)
      yrs_normal_early_midlife = sum(bmi_category == "Normal" & xage > 25 & xage <= 40, na.rm = TRUE),
      yrs_overweight_early_midlife = sum(bmi_category == "Overweight" & xage > 25 & xage <= 40, na.rm = TRUE),
      yrs_obese_early_midlife = sum(bmi_category == "Obese" & xage > 25 & xage <= 40, na.rm = TRUE),

      # Years in each BMI category - Late midlife (41-60)
      yrs_normal_late_midlife = sum(bmi_category == "Normal" & xage > 40 & xage <= 60, na.rm = TRUE),
      yrs_overweight_late_midlife = sum(bmi_category == "Overweight" & xage > 40 & xage <= 60, na.rm = TRUE),
      yrs_obese_late_midlife = sum(bmi_category == "Obese" & xage > 40 & xage <= 60, na.rm = TRUE)
    ) %>%
    right_join(df.model, by = "ncdsid")

# Define the 6 BMI predictors to loop through
bmi_predictors <- c(
  "mean_bmi_early_life",
  "mean_bmi_early_midlife",
  "mean_bmi_late_midlife",
  "yrs_normal_earlylife",   
  "yrs_overweight_earlylife",
  "yrs_obese_earlylife",
  "yrs_normal_early_midlife",
  "yrs_overweight_early_midlife",
  "yrs_obese_early_midlife",          
  "yrs_normal_late_midlife",
  "yrs_overweight_late_midlife",
  "yrs_obese_late_midlife"
)

# Run separate time period models and store each model in a list
models_list.timeperiod <- map(bmi_predictors, function(bmi_var) {
    all_covs <- c(bmi_var, covs)
    formula <- reformulate(all_covs, response = "loghsCRP")

    df.timeperiod %>%
      select(all_of(c("loghsCRP", all_covs))) %>%
      rename(exposure = all_of(bmi_var)) %>%  # Rename to generic "exposure"
      lm(reformulate(c("exposure", covs), response = "loghsCRP"), data = .)
  })

saveRDS(models_list.timeperiod, file = here("Data", "Models", "models_list.timeperiod.rds"))

```

```{r}
#| label: tbl-s5a-mean
#| tbl-cap: "Separate adjusted regressions of log(hsCRP) on period-specific mean BMI."

cov_labels <- list(
  exposure ~ "BMI Exposure",
  sex ~ "Sex",
  sweep10_age ~ "Age at hsCRP measurement (years)",
  educ_qual ~ "Educational qualification",
  father_bmi ~ "Father's BMI",
  mother_bmi ~ "Mother's BMI",
  n646 ~ "Birthweight",
  n607 ~ "Persons per room",
  n2383 ~ "Father's occupation"
)

tbl_merge(
    tbls = map(models_list.timeperiod[1:3], ~ .x %>%
      tbl_regression(
        intercept = FALSE,
        label = cov_labels
      ) %>%
      add_significance_stars() %>%
      bold_p() %>%
      bold_labels()
    ),
    tab_spanner = c(
      "**Mean BMI <br> (7-25)**",
      "**Mean BMI <br> (26-40)**",
      "**Mean BMI <br> (41-56)**"
    )
  ) %>%
    modify_table_body(
      ~ .x %>%
        mutate(
          is_exposure = variable == "exposure",
          is_glance = row_type == "glance_statistic"
        ) %>%
        arrange(desc(is_exposure), is_glance) %>%
        select(-is_exposure, -is_glance)
    )
```

```{r}
#| label: tbl-s5b-yrs-early
#| tbl-cap: "Separate adjusted regressions of log(hsCRP) on years in each BMI category during early life (ages 7-25)."

tbl_merge(
    tbls = map(models_list.timeperiod[4:6], ~ .x %>%
      tbl_regression(
        intercept = FALSE,
        label = cov_labels
      ) %>%
      add_significance_stars() %>%
      bold_p() %>%
      bold_labels()
    ),
    tab_spanner = c(
      "**Yrs Normal <br> (7-25)**",
      "**Yrs Overweight <br> (7-25)**",
      "**Yrs Obese <br> (7-25)**"
    )
  ) %>%
    modify_table_body(
      ~ .x %>%
        mutate(
          is_exposure = variable == "exposure",
          is_glance = row_type == "glance_statistic"
        ) %>%
        arrange(desc(is_exposure), is_glance) %>%
        select(-is_exposure, -is_glance)
    )
```

```{r}
#| label: tbl-s5c-yrs-earlymid
#| tbl-cap: "Separate adjusted regressions of log(hsCRP) on years in each BMI category during early midlife (ages 26-40)."

tbl_merge(
    tbls = map(models_list.timeperiod[7:9], ~ .x %>%
      tbl_regression(
        intercept = FALSE,
        label = cov_labels
      ) %>%
      add_significance_stars() %>%
      bold_p() %>%
      bold_labels()
    ),
    tab_spanner = c(
      "**Yrs Normal <br> (26-40)**",
      "**Yrs Overweight <br> (26-40)**",
      "**Yrs Obese <br> (26-40)**"
    )
  ) %>%
    modify_table_body(
      ~ .x %>%
        mutate(
          is_exposure = variable == "exposure",
          is_glance = row_type == "glance_statistic"
        ) %>%
        arrange(desc(is_exposure), is_glance) %>%
        select(-is_exposure, -is_glance)
    )
```

```{r}
#| label: tbl-s5d-yrs-latemid
#| tbl-cap: "Separate adjusted regressions of log(hsCRP) on years in each BMI category during late midlife (ages 41-56)."

tbl_merge(
    tbls = map(models_list.timeperiod[10:12], ~ .x %>%
      tbl_regression(
        intercept = FALSE,
        label = cov_labels
      ) %>%
      add_significance_stars() %>%
      bold_p() %>%
      bold_labels()
    ),
    tab_spanner = c(
      "**Yrs Normal <br> (41-56)**",
      "**Yrs Overweight <br> (41-56)**",
      "**Yrs Obese <br> (41-56)**"
    )
  ) %>%
    modify_table_body(
      ~ .x %>%
        mutate(
          is_exposure = variable == "exposure",
          is_glance = row_type == "glance_statistic"
        ) %>%
        arrange(desc(is_exposure), is_glance) %>%
        select(-is_exposure, -is_glance)
    )
```

## Conditional Models of Time Periods

```{r}                                                                          
#| label: tbl-s4-conditional
#| tbl-cap: "Conditional adjusted regressions of log(hsCRP) with mean BMI from multiple life periods entered jointly, using a stepwise inclusion strategy to assess conditional period-specific effects."

# Define the 3 staggered BMI predictor combinations
bmi_predictor_sets <- list(
    "Early Life" = "mean_bmi_early_life",
    "Early + Early Midlife" = c("mean_bmi_early_life", "mean_bmi_early_midlife"),
    "All Three Periods" = c("mean_bmi_early_life", "mean_bmi_early_midlife", "mean_bmi_late_midlife")
  )

# Run conditional time period models and store each table in a list
models_list.timeperiod.staggered.conditional <- map(bmi_predictor_sets, function(bmi_vars) {
    all_covs <- c(bmi_vars, covs)
    formula <- reformulate(all_covs, response = "loghsCRP")

    df.timeperiod %>%
      select(all_of(c("loghsCRP", all_covs))) %>%
      lm(formula, data = .)
  })

saveRDS(models_list.timeperiod.staggered.conditional, file = here("Data", "Models", "models_list.timeperiod.conditional.rds"))

```

```{r}
cov_labels <- list(
  mean_bmi_early_life ~ "Mean BMI (7-25)",
  mean_bmi_early_midlife ~ "Mean BMI (26-40)",
  mean_bmi_late_midlife ~ "Mean BMI (41-56)",
  sex ~ "Sex",
  sweep10_age ~ "Age at hsCRP measurement (years)",
  educ_qual ~ "Educational qualification",
  father_bmi ~ "Father's BMI",
  mother_bmi ~ "Mother's BMI",
  n646 ~ "Birthweight",
  n607 ~ "Persons per room",
  n2383 ~ "Father's occupation"
)

tbl_merge(
    tbls = map2(models_list.timeperiod.staggered.conditional, bmi_predictor_sets, function(mod, bmi_vars) {
      # Filter labels to only include variables in this model
      model_vars <- c(bmi_vars, covs)
      model_labels <- cov_labels[sapply(cov_labels, function(x) as.character(x[[2]]) %in% model_vars)]

      mod %>%
        tbl_regression(
          intercept = FALSE,
          label = model_labels
        ) %>%
        add_significance_stars(hide_ci = FALSE) %>%
        bold_p() %>%
        bold_labels()
    }),
    tab_spanner = names(bmi_predictor_sets) %>% paste0("**", ., "**")
  ) %>%
    modify_table_body(
      ~ .x %>%
        mutate(
          is_bmi = variable %in% c("mean_bmi_early_life", "mean_bmi_early_midlife", "mean_bmi_late_midlife"),
          is_glance = row_type == "glance_statistic"
        ) %>%
        arrange(desc(is_bmi), is_glance) %>%
        select(-is_bmi, -is_glance)
    )
```

<hr style="border: 2px solid black;">

# Multiple Informant Model Using General Estimating Equations

The multiple informant approach estimates associations between exposures at different life stages and a single outcome measure in a unified regression framework, treating synthetic copies of the outcome as repeated measures.[@horton1999; @litman2006; @sanchez2011] Estimation can be conducted using generalized estimating equations or maximum likelihood approaches.[@bather2023; @litman2007] Including the period indicators, BMI, and their interaction together quantifies differences in the exposure association across periods.

Here, the data are reshaped to long format, with each participant contributing three rows (one per period: early life, early midlife, and late midlife). The GEE model includes a period-by-BMI interaction term, enabling a formal test of whether the association between BMI and log(hsCRP) varies across life stages. An independence working correlation structure is specified, as the repeated measures here represent summary statistics from different life windows. @tbl-s5-gee reports the estimated main effects of BMI and period, the interaction terms, and all covariates. Linear hypothesis tests are conducted to compute the BMI--inflammation association in each period, with results presented in the main text.

```{r}
library(geepack)
library(geeM)

# Reshape the data to long format for GEE, keeping only necessary variables and ensuring complete cases for hsCRP and covariates
df.gee <- df.timeperiod %>%
  select(ncdsid, loghsCRP,
         mean_bmi_early_life,
         mean_bmi_early_midlife,
         mean_bmi_late_midlife,
         all_of(covs)) %>%
  drop_na(loghsCRP, all_of(covs)) %>%
  group_by(ncdsid) %>%
  nest() %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  unnest() %>%
  pivot_longer(
    cols = starts_with("mean_bmi"),
    names_to = "window",
    names_prefix = "mean_bmi_",
    values_to = "bmi"
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    ncdsid_numeric = as.numeric(factor(ncdsid))
  ) %>%
  relocate(ncdsid_numeric, .after = "ncdsid")

# Run the gee model
gee_model <- geeglm(
    loghsCRP ~ window * bmi + sex + sweep10_age + educ_qual + father_bmi + mother_bmi + n646 + n607 + n2383,
    id = ncdsid_numeric,
    data = df.gee,
    family = gaussian(),
    corstr = "independence"
  )

saveRDS(gee_model, file = here("Data", "Models", "gee_model.rds"))

```

```{r}
#| label: tbl-s5-gee
#| tbl-cap: "GEE regression of log(hsCRP) on period-specific BMI with a period-by-BMI interaction, testing whether the BMI--inflammation association differs across early life, early midlife, and late midlife."
gee_labels <- list(
  window ~ "Period",
  bmi ~ "BMI",
  `window:bmi` ~ "Period × BMI",
  sex ~ "Sex",
  sweep10_age ~ "Age at hsCRP measurement (years)",
  educ_qual ~ "Educational qualification",
  father_bmi ~ "Father's BMI",
  mother_bmi ~ "Mother's BMI",
  n646 ~ "Birthweight",
  n607 ~ "Persons per room",
  n2383 ~ "Father's occupation"
)

gee_model %>%
  tbl_regression(
    label = gee_labels,
    include = c(window, bmi, `window:bmi`, sex, sweep10_age, educ_qual,
                father_bmi, mother_bmi, n646, n607, n2383)
  ) %>%
  add_significance_stars(hide_ci = FALSE) %>%
  bold_p() %>%
  bold_labels()
```

<hr style="border: 2px solid black;">

# Sequence Analysis

Sequence analysis represents life course exposure as an ordered series of categorical states and groups individuals based on similarities in these state sequences using distance metrics and clustering algorithms.[@aisenbrey2010; @elsenburg2024] The resulting sequence clusters can then be used as categorical predictors in regression models to estimate differences in outcomes across trajectory patterns. Pairwise dissimilarities between sequences were computed using optimal matching, and Ward's hierarchical clustering was applied to the resulting distance matrix to identify groups of individuals with similar BMI state trajectories. Analyses were conducted using the `TraMineR` and `cluster` packages in R.[@gabadinho2025] Sequence index plots are presented for solutions ranging from 2 to 6 clusters (@fig-s3-seq-2cl through @fig-s7-seq-6cl), along with the full unpartitioned sample (@fig-s2-seq-all). @fig-s8-cluster-quality displays five cluster quality metrics (ASW, Calinski-Harabasz, Hubert's Gamma, Point Biserial Correlation, and R-squared) across cluster solutions to guide the choice of the optimal number of groups. Based on these diagnostics, a 3-cluster solution is selected and the resulting trajectory class membership is used as a categorical predictor of log(hsCRP) in a fully adjusted regression model (@tbl-s6-sequence).

```{r}
library(TraMineR)
library(cluster)
library(ggseqplot)

# Define the sequence data for BMI categories across ages, pivoting to wide format and merging with the main dataset
df.sequence  <- df.bmi_prediction %>%
  select(ncdsid, xage, bmi_category) %>%
  pivot_wider(
    names_from = xage,
    names_prefix = "age_",
    values_from = bmi_category
  ) %>% 
  right_join(df.model, by = "ncdsid")

bmi_levels <- c("Normal", "Overweight", "Obese")

# Create the sequence object with defined labels and colors
bmi_seq <- df.sequence %>%
  select(starts_with("age_")) %>%
  seqdef(
    alphabet = bmi_levels,
    states = bmi_levels,
    labels = bmi_levels,
    cpal = c("forestgreen", "goldenrod", "firebrick")
  )

# Define the distance matrix using the optimal matching method and perform hierarchical clustering using ward's method
dist.om1 <- seqdist(bmi_seq, method = "OM", indel = 1, sm = "TRATE")
clusters <- agnes(dist.om1, diss = TRUE, method = "ward")
```


```{r}
#| label: fig-s2-seq-all
#| fig-cap: "Sequence index plot displaying each individual's BMI category trajectory (Normal, Overweight, Obese) across ages 7--56, sorted by onset of first state transition."
ggseqiplot(bmi_seq, sortv = "from.start")
```

```{r}
#| label: fig-s3-seq-2cl
#| fig-cap: "Sequence index plots of BMI category trajectories stratified by a 2-cluster solution from hierarchical clustering with optimal matching distances."
# 2 Clusters
cl1.2 <- cutree(clusters, k = 2)
cl1.2fac <- factor(cl1.2, labels = paste("Type", 1:2))
# seqIplot(bmi_seq, group = cl1.2fac, sortv = "from.start")

ggseqiplot(bmi_seq, group = cl1.2fac, sortv = "from.start")

```

```{r}
#| label: fig-s4-seq-3cl
#| fig-cap: "Sequence index plots of BMI category trajectories stratified by a 3-cluster solution, identifying Normal BMI, Normal-to-Overweight, and Overweight-to-Obese trajectory groups."
# 3 Clusters
cl1.3 <- cutree(clusters, k = 3)
cl1.3fac <- factor(cl1.3, labels = paste("Type", 1:3))
# seqIplot(bmi_seq, group = cl1.3fac, sortv = "from.start")

ggseqiplot(bmi_seq, group = cl1.3fac, sortv = "from.start")       

```

```{r}
#| label: fig-s5-seq-4cl
#| fig-cap: "Sequence index plots of BMI category trajectories stratified by a 4-cluster solution from hierarchical clustering."
# 4 Clusters
cl1.4 <- cutree(clusters, k = 4)
cl1.4fac <- factor(cl1.4, labels = paste("Type", 1:4))
# seqIplot(bmi_seq, group = cl1.4fac, sortv = "from.start")

ggseqiplot(bmi_seq, group = cl1.4fac, sortv = "from.start")
```

```{r}
#| label: fig-s6-seq-5cl
#| fig-cap: "Sequence index plots of BMI category trajectories stratified by a 5-cluster solution from hierarchical clustering."
# 5 Clusters
cl1.5 <- cutree(clusters, k = 5)
cl1.5fac <- factor(cl1.5, labels = paste("Type", 1:5))
# seqIplot(bmi_seq, group = cl1.5fac, sortv = "from.start")

ggseqiplot(bmi_seq, group = cl1.5fac, sortv = "from.start")
```

```{r}
#| label: fig-s7-seq-6cl
#| fig-cap: "Sequence index plots of BMI category trajectories stratified by a 6-cluster solution from hierarchical clustering."
# 6 Clusters
cl1.6 <- cutree(clusters, k = 6)
cl1.6fac <- factor(cl1.6, labels = paste("Type", 1:6))
# seqIplot(bmi_seq, group = cl1.6fac, sortv = "from.start",with.legend = FALSE)

ggseqiplot(bmi_seq, group = cl1.6fac, sortv = "from.start")
```

## Determining Number of Clusters

```{r}
#| label: fig-s8-cluster-quality
#| fig-cap: "Cluster quality metrics (ASW, Calinski-Harabasz, Hubert's Gamma, Point Biserial Correlation, and R-squared) across 2- to 6-cluster solutions to guide selection of the optimal number of BMI trajectory groups."
library(WeightedCluster)

# Create a named list of clustering results
cluster_list <- list(
  "2" = cl1.2,
  "3" = cl1.3,
  "4" = cl1.4,
  "5" = cl1.5,
  "6" = cl1.6
)

# Apply wcClusterQuality and extract key metrics
cluster_metrics <- map_dfr(names(cluster_list), function(k) {
  res <- wcClusterQuality(dist.om1, cluster_list[[k]])
  tibble(
    k = as.integer(k),
    ASW = res$stats["ASW"],
    CH = res$stats["CH"],
    R2 = res$stats["R2"],
    PBC = res$stats["PBC"],
    HG = res$stats["HG"]
  )
})

# Reshape for plotting
long_metrics <- cluster_metrics %>%
  pivot_longer(-k, names_to = "metric", values_to = "value")

# Plot
ggplot(long_metrics, aes(x = k, y = value)) +
  geom_line() +
  geom_point() +
  facet_wrap(~ metric, scales = "free_y") +
  labs(x = "Number of clusters", y = "Value", title = "Cluster Quality Metrics") +
  theme_minimal()

```

-   ASW (Average Silhouette Width): Measures how similar each observation is to its own cluster compared to other clusters; values closer to 1 indicate better clustering.

-   CH (Calinski-Harabasz Index / Pseudo F Statistic): Ratio of between-cluster variance to within-cluster variance based on the distance matrix.; higher values suggest more distinct clusters.

-   HG (Hubert’s Gamma): Measures the correlation between the distances in the data and a binary matrix indicating whether points are in the same cluster (0) or not (1); values closer to 1 reflect stronger clustering structure.

-   PBC (Point Biserial Correlation): Measures correlation between the pairwise distances and a binary cluster membership indicator (same cluster vs. different cluster); higher is better.

-   R2 (Explained Discrepancy): Indicates the proportion of total variation explained by the clustering; values near 1 are ideal.

## Predicting Log hsCRP with 4 Sequence Trajectories

```{r}
# Merge the 3-cluster variable back into the main dataset for regression analysis 
df.sequence.pred <- df.sequence %>%
  select(ncdsid) %>%
  cbind(cl1.3) %>%
  rename(TrajectoryClass = cl1.3) %>% 
  mutate(TrajectoryClass = factor(TrajectoryClass,
                                  levels = c(3, 1, 2),
                                  labels = c(
                                    "Normal BMI", "Normal-Overweight", "Overweight-Obese"
                                  ))) %>%
  right_join(df.model, by = "ncdsid")

bmi_var <- "TrajectoryClass"
outcomes <- c("loghsCRP")

model.sequence <- map(outcomes, function(outcome) {
  all_covs <- c(bmi_var, covs)
  formula <- reformulate(all_covs, response = outcome)
  
  df.sequence.pred %>%
    select(all_of(c(outcome, all_covs))) %>%
    lm(formula, data = .)
})

saveRDS(model.sequence, file = here("Data", "Models", "model.sequence.rds"))
```

```{r}
#| label: tbl-s6-sequence
#| tbl-cap: "Adjusted linear regression of log(hsCRP) on 3-cluster BMI sequence membership, with the Normal BMI cluster as the reference group."
model.sequence %>%
  .[[1]] %>%
  tbl_regression(
    label = list(
      TrajectoryClass ~ "Sequence Class",
      sex ~ "Sex",
      sweep10_age ~ "Age at hsCRP measurement (years)",
      educ_qual ~ "Educational qualification",
      father_bmi ~ "Father's BMI",
      mother_bmi ~ "Mother's BMI",
      n646 ~ "Birthweight",
      n607 ~ "Persons per room",
      n2383 ~ "Father's occupation"
    )
  ) %>%
  add_significance_stars(hide_ci = FALSE) %>%
  bold_p() %>%
  bold_labels()
```

<hr style="border: 2px solid black;">

# Latent Class Trajectory Modeling

Latent class trajectory modeling refers to a family of model-based approaches that identify unobserved subgroups of individuals following similar exposure trajectories over time using finite mixture models.[@herle2020; @vandernest2020] These models estimate class-specific trajectory shapes and assign individuals probabilistically to latent trajectory classes. Estimation is typically conducted using maximum likelihood, and distal outcomes assessed using a range of approaches.[@bakk2021; @nylundgibson2019]

The growth mixture model (GMM) is a type of latent class trajectory model that allows individual-level random effects within classes, thereby relaxing the assumption that all individuals within a class follow identical trajectories. We additionally allowed class-specific residual variance-covariance structures, enabling within-class variability in BMI to differ across latent classes. The large number of repeated time points in the observed BMI data substantially increased model parameterization, motivating the use of decade-based age averages. Using the `lcmm` package in R [@proustlima2017], growth mixture models were fit with three functional forms for the time trend: linear, logarithmic, and quantile spline, and with varying numbers of latent classes (1 to 6). Spline models used three internal knots at quantiles of the age distribution to allow flexible nonlinear shapes. Model fit was evaluated using log-likelihood, AIC, BIC, entropy, class proportions, and substantive interpretability. @tbl-s7-fit compares model fit statistics across all specifications to guide the selection of the best-fitting model. A 4-class logarithmic model is selected based on these criteria.

To relate trajectory class membership to inflammation, two approaches are employed. First, a naive 3-step method assigns individuals to their most likely class and enters this assignment directly as a categorical predictor of log(hsCRP) (@tbl-s7-lca). This approach ignores classification uncertainty, potentially biasing effect estimates toward zero. Second, a maximum likelihood (ML) 3-step method is implemented manually based on Vermunt 2017, incorporating the posterior class probability matrix and the classification error matrix to account for misclassification when estimating class-specific means of the covariate-adjusted outcome (@tbl-s8-lca-ml). The ML approach provides covariate-adjusted contrasts between trajectory classes with correct standard errors.

```{r}
library(lcmm)

# Define the data for latent class growth modeling, calculating mean BMI within defined age groups and merging with covariates
df.lgm <- df.bmi_prediction %>%
  right_join(df.model, by = "ncdsid") %>% 
  mutate(
    age_group = case_when(
      xage > 6  & xage <= 20 ~ 13.5,
      xage > 20 & xage <= 30 ~ 25,
      xage > 30 & xage <= 40 ~ 35,
      xage > 40 & xage <= 50 ~ 45,
      xage > 50 & xage <= 60 ~ 55,
      TRUE ~ NA_real_
    )
  ) %>%
  group_by(ncdsid, age_group) %>%
  summarise(mean_bmi = mean(pred_bmi, na.rm = TRUE)) %>%
  left_join(  # Reattach time-invariant covariates
    df.model %>%
      select(ncdsid, loghsCRP, all_of(covs)) %>% 
      mutate(n607 = as_factor(n607)) %>%
      zap_label(),
    by = "ncdsid"
  ) %>% 
  mutate(ncdsid = ncdsid %>%
           str_extract("\\d+") %>%
           as.numeric()) %>% 
  ungroup() %>% 
  as.data.frame()
```

```{r}
set.seed(1234)

# Fit latent class growth models with 1 to 4 classes, using a linear specification for age and allowing for class-specific trajectories

# gmmd.bmi.cont.1.lin <- lcmm::hlme(fixed = mean_bmi ~ 1 + age_group ,
#            random = ~1 + age_group,
#            subject = "ncdsid",
#            ng = 1,
#            idiag = FALSE,
#            data = df.lgm,
#            verbose=F)

# gmmd.bmi.cont.2.lin <- lcmm::hlme(fixed = mean_bmi ~ 1 + age_group,
#            mixture = ~ 1 + age_group,
#            random = ~1 + age_group,
#            subject = "ncdsid",
#            ng = 2,
#            nwg = T,
#            idiag = FALSE,
#            data = df.lgm,
#            B=gmmd.bmi.cont.1.lin,
#            verbose=F)

# gmmd.bmi.cont.3.lin <- hlme(fixed = mean_bmi ~ 1 + age_group,
#            mixture = ~ 1 + age_group,
#            random = ~1 + age_group,
#            subject = "ncdsid",
#            ng = 3,
#            nwg = T,
#            idiag = FALSE,
#            data = df.lgm,
#            B=gmmd.bmi.cont.1.lin,
#            verbose=F)

# gmmd.bmi.cont.4.lin <- hlme(fixed = mean_bmi ~ 1 + age_group,
#            mixture = ~ 1 + age_group,
#            random = ~1 + age_group,
#            subject = "ncdsid",
#            ng = 4,
#            nwg = T,
#            idiag = FALSE,
#            data = df.lgm,
#            B=gmmd.bmi.cont.1.lin,
#            verbose=F)

# saveRDS(gmmd.bmi.cont.1.lin,here("Data/gmmd.bmi.cont.1.lin.rds"))
# saveRDS(gmmd.bmi.cont.2.lin,here("Data/gmmd.bmi.cont.2.lin.rds"))
# saveRDS(gmmd.bmi.cont.3.lin,here("Data/gmmd.bmi.cont.3.lin.rds"))
# saveRDS(gmmd.bmi.cont.4.lin,here("Data/gmmd.bmi.cont.4.lin.rds"))

gmmd.bmi.cont.1.lin <- readRDS(here("Data/gmmd.bmi.cont.1.lin.rds"))
gmmd.bmi.cont.2.lin <- readRDS(here("Data/gmmd.bmi.cont.2.lin.rds"))
gmmd.bmi.cont.3.lin <- readRDS(here("Data/gmmd.bmi.cont.3.lin.rds"))
gmmd.bmi.cont.4.lin <- readRDS(here("Data/gmmd.bmi.cont.4.lin.rds"))

# Create a summary table of model fit statistics for the linear models
gmmfit.cont.lin <- lcmm::summarytable(
  gmmd.bmi.cont.1.lin, gmmd.bmi.cont.2.lin, gmmd.bmi.cont.3.lin, gmmd.bmi.cont.4.lin,
  which = c("G", "loglik", "AIC", "BIC", "SABIC", "entropy", "%class"),
  display = FALSE
) %>%
  as_tibble() %>%
  mutate(entropy = round(entropy, digits = 2),
         Form = "Linear")


```

```{r}
set.seed(1234)

# Fit latent class growth models with 1 to 6 classes, using a log specification for age and allowing for class-specific trajectories

# gmmd.bmi.cont.1.log <- lcmm::hlme(fixed = mean_bmi ~ 1 + log(age_group) ,
#            random = ~1 + age_group,
#            subject = "ncdsid",
#            ng = 1,
#            idiag = FALSE,
#            data = df.lgm,
#            verbose=F)

# gmmd.bmi.cont.2.log <- lcmm::hlme(fixed = mean_bmi ~ 1 + log(age_group) ,
#            mixture = ~ 1 + log(age_group) ,
#            random = ~1 + age_group,
#            subject = "ncdsid",
#            ng = 2,
#            nwg = T,
#            idiag = FALSE,
#            data = df.lgm,
#            B=gmmd.bmi.cont.1.log,
#            verbose=F)


# gmmd.bmi.cont.3.log <- hlme(fixed = mean_bmi ~ 1 + log(age_group) ,
#            mixture = ~ 1 + log(age_group) ,
#            random = ~1 + age_group,
#            subject = "ncdsid",
#            ng = 3,
#            nwg = T,
#            idiag = FALSE,
#            data = df.lgm,
#            B=gmmd.bmi.cont.1.log,
#            verbose=F)

# gmmd.bmi.cont.4.log <- hlme(fixed = mean_bmi ~ 1 + log(age_group) ,
#            mixture = ~ 1 + log(age_group) ,
#            random = ~1 + age_group,
#            subject = "ncdsid",
#            ng = 4,
#            nwg = T,
#            idiag = FALSE,
#            data = df.lgm,
#            B=gmmd.bmi.cont.1.log,
#            verbose=F)

# gmmd.bmi.cont.5.log <- hlme(fixed = mean_bmi ~ 1 + log(age_group) ,
#            mixture = ~ 1 + log(age_group) ,
#            random = ~1 + age_group,
#            subject = "ncdsid",
#            ng = 5,
#            nwg = T,
#            idiag = FALSE,
#            data = df.lgm,
#            B=gmmd.bmi.cont.1.log,
#            verbose=F)

# gmmd.bmi.cont.6.log <- hlme(fixed = mean_bmi ~ 1 + log(age_group) ,
#            mixture = ~ 1 + log(age_group) ,
#            random = ~1 + age_group,
#            subject = "ncdsid",
#            ng = 6,
#            nwg = T,
#            idiag = FALSE,
#            data = df.lgm,
#            B=gmmd.bmi.cont.1.log,
#            verbose=F)

# saveRDS(gmmd.bmi.cont.1.log,here("Data/gmmd.bmi.cont.1.log.rds"))
# saveRDS(gmmd.bmi.cont.2.log,here("Data/gmmd.bmi.cont.2.log.rds"))
# saveRDS(gmmd.bmi.cont.3.log,here("Data/gmmd.bmi.cont.3.log.rds"))
# saveRDS(gmmd.bmi.cont.4.log,here("Data/gmmd.bmi.cont.4.log.rds"))
# saveRDS(gmmd.bmi.cont.5.log,here("Data/gmmd.bmi.cont.5.log.rds"))
# saveRDS(gmmd.bmi.cont.6.log,here("Data/gmmd.bmi.cont.6.log.rds"))

gmmd.bmi.cont.1.log <- readRDS(here("Data/gmmd.bmi.cont.1.log.rds"))
gmmd.bmi.cont.2.log <- readRDS(here("Data/gmmd.bmi.cont.2.log.rds"))
gmmd.bmi.cont.3.log <- readRDS(here("Data/gmmd.bmi.cont.3.log.rds"))
gmmd.bmi.cont.4.log <- readRDS(here("Data/gmmd.bmi.cont.4.log.rds"))
gmmd.bmi.cont.5.log <- readRDS(here("Data/gmmd.bmi.cont.5.log.rds"))
gmmd.bmi.cont.6.log <- readRDS(here("Data/gmmd.bmi.cont.6.log.rds"))

gmmfit.cont.log <- lcmm::summarytable(
  gmmd.bmi.cont.1.log, gmmd.bmi.cont.2.log, gmmd.bmi.cont.3.log,
  gmmd.bmi.cont.4.log, gmmd.bmi.cont.5.log, gmmd.bmi.cont.6.log,
  which = c("G", "loglik", "AIC", "BIC", "SABIC", "entropy", "%class"),
  display = FALSE
) %>%
  as_tibble() %>%
  mutate(entropy = round(entropy, digits = 2),
         Form = "Log")


```

```{r}
set.seed(1234)

# Fit latent class growth models with 1 to 4 classes, using a spline specification for age and allowing for class-specific trajectories

# gmmd.bmi.cont.1 <- lcmm::lcmm(fixed = mean_bmi ~ 1 + age_group ,
#            link='3-quant-splines',
#            random = ~1 + age_group,
#            subject = "ncdsid",
#            ng = 1,
#            idiag = FALSE,
#            data = df.lgm,
#            verbose=F)

# gmmd.bmi.cont.2 <- lcmm::lcmm(fixed = mean_bmi ~ 1 + age_group,
#           link='3-quant-splines',
#            mixture = ~ 1 + age_group,
#            random = ~1 + age_group,
#            subject = "ncdsid",
#            ng = 2,
#            nwg = T,
#            idiag = FALSE,
#            data = df.lgm,
#            B=gmmd.bmi.cont.1,
#            verbose=F)


# gmmd.bmi.cont.3 <- lcmm(fixed = mean_bmi ~ 1 + age_group,
#                         link='3-quant-splines',
#            mixture = ~ 1 + age_group,
#            random = ~1 + age_group,
#            subject = "ncdsid",
#            ng = 3,
#            nwg = T,
#            idiag = FALSE,
#            data = df.lgm,
#            B=gmmd.bmi.cont.1,
#            verbose=F)

# gmmd.bmi.cont.4 <- lcmm(fixed = mean_bmi ~ 1 + age_group,
#                         link='3-quant-splines',
#            mixture = ~ 1 + age_group,
#            random = ~1 + age_group,
#            subject = "ncdsid",
#            ng = 4,
#            nwg = T,
#            idiag = FALSE,
#            data = df.lgm,
#            B=gmmd.bmi.cont.1,
#            verbose=F)

# saveRDS(gmmd.bmi.cont.1,here("Data/gmmd.bmi.cont.1.rds"))
# saveRDS(gmmd.bmi.cont.2,here("Data/gmmd.bmi.cont.2.rds"))
# saveRDS(gmmd.bmi.cont.3,here("Data/gmmd.bmi.cont.3.rds"))
# saveRDS(gmmd.bmi.cont.4,here("Data/gmmd.bmi.cont.4.rds"))

gmmd.bmi.cont.1 <- readRDS(here("Data/gmmd.bmi.cont.1.rds"))
gmmd.bmi.cont.2 <- readRDS(here("Data/gmmd.bmi.cont.2.rds"))
gmmd.bmi.cont.3 <- readRDS(here("Data/gmmd.bmi.cont.3.rds"))
gmmd.bmi.cont.4 <- readRDS(here("Data/gmmd.bmi.cont.4.rds"))

gmmfit.cont.spline <- lcmm::summarytable(
  gmmd.bmi.cont.1, gmmd.bmi.cont.2, gmmd.bmi.cont.3, gmmd.bmi.cont.4,
  which = c("G", "loglik", "AIC", "BIC", "SABIC", "entropy", "%class"),
  display = FALSE
) %>%
  as_tibble() %>%
  mutate(entropy = round(entropy, digits = 2),
         Form = "Spline")

```

## Latent Class Trajectory Model Fit

```{r}
#| label: tbl-s7-fit
#| tbl-cap: "Model fit statistics (log-likelihood, AIC, BIC, SABIC, and entropy) for latent class trajectory models with linear, logarithmic, and spline functional forms across varying numbers of classes."
bind_rows(gmmfit.cont.lin, gmmfit.cont.log, gmmfit.cont.spline) %>%
    relocate(Form) %>%
    gt(groupname_col = "Form") %>%
    tab_header(title = "Growth Mixture Model Fit Comparison") %>%
    tab_options(
      heading.background.color = "gray",
      heading.title.font.size = px(16),
      heading.title.font.weight = "bold",
      table.font.size = px(12)
    ) %>%
    cols_label(
      G = "Functional Form",
      loglik = "Log Likelihood",
      entropy = "Relative Entropy",
      BIC = "BIC",
      SABIC = "SABIC"
    ) %>%
    tab_style(
      style = list(
        cell_fill(color = "lightgray"),
        cell_text(weight = "bold")
      ),
      locations = cells_body(
        rows = Form == "Log" & G == 4
      )
    )
```

## Predicting Log hsCRP with 3-class Logarithmic LCTM (Naive 3-step method - No Class Uncertainity)

```{r}

bmi_var <- "TrajectoryClass"
outcomes <- c("loghsCRP")

# Run LCTM models
model.lca <- map(outcomes, function(outcome) {
  all_covs <- c(bmi_var, covs)
  formula <- reformulate(all_covs, response = outcome)
  
gmmd.bmi.cont.4.log$pprob %>%
  select(ncdsid, class) %>% 
  mutate(
    TrajectoryClass = factor(class, 
                           levels = c(1, 2, 3, 4),
                           labels = c("Normal BMI",
                                      "Slightly elevated BMI", 
                                      "High BMI",
                                      "High BMI and Increasing"
                                      ))
  ) %>%
  right_join(df.lgm, by = 'ncdsid') %>% 
    select(-age_group) %>% 
    distinct() %>% 
    select(all_of(c(outcome, all_covs))) %>%
    lm(formula, data = .) 
})

saveRDS(model.lca, file = here("Data", "Models", "model.LCA.rds"))
```

```{r}
#| label: tbl-s7-lca
#| tbl-cap: "Adjusted linear regression of log(hsCRP) on 4-class logarithmic LCTM membership using naive modal class assignment (no correction for classification uncertainty)."
model.lca %>%
  .[[1]] %>%
  tbl_regression(
    label = list(
      TrajectoryClass ~ "Trajectory Class",
      sex ~ "Sex",
      sweep10_age ~ "Age at hsCRP measurement (years)",
      educ_qual ~ "Educational qualification",
      father_bmi ~ "Father's BMI",
      mother_bmi ~ "Mother's BMI",
      n646 ~ "Birthweight",
      n607 ~ "Persons per room",
      n2383 ~ "Father's occupation"
    )
  ) %>%
  add_significance_stars(hide_ci = FALSE) %>%
  bold_p() %>%
  bold_labels()
```

## Predicting Log hsCRP with 3-Class Logarithmic LCTM Using Manual ML Method

```{r}
# Implementing the ML-3 Step Method Manually to Account for Classification Uncertainty

# Step 1. Join with posterior class probabilities from hlme
distal_var <- "loghsCRP"

df_person <- df.lgm %>%
  select(ncdsid, all_of(distal_var), all_of(covs)) %>%
  distinct()

pp <- gmmd.bmi.cont.4.log$pprob %>%
  select(ncdsid, class, starts_with("prob"))

# how many classes
Tcl <- length(grep("^prob", names(pp)))
prob_cols <- paste0("prob", 1:Tcl)

analysis_df <- pp %>%
  inner_join(df_person, by = "ncdsid") %>%
  drop_na(!!sym(distal_var))

# posterior matrix tau_it = P(X=t | Y_i)
tau <- as.matrix(analysis_df[, prob_cols])  # N x T
Wobs <- analysis_df$class                   # observed assigned class W_i
N    <- nrow(analysis_df)
Tcl  <- ncol(tau)

# Step 2. Classification error matrix P(W = s | X = t)

# N x T indicator matrix: I(W_i = s)
W_mat <- sapply(1:Tcl, function(s) as.numeric(Wobs == s))  # N x T

R <- matrix(NA_real_, nrow = Tcl, ncol = Tcl,
            dimnames = list(paste0("X", 1:Tcl),
                            paste0("W", 1:Tcl)))

for (t in 1:Tcl) {
  denom_t <- sum(tau[, t])
  for (s in 1:Tcl) {
    numer_ts <- sum(tau[, t] * W_mat[, s])
    R[t, s] <- numer_ts / denom_t
  }
}

# R
# rowSums(R)  # should be ~ 1 for each t

pi_hat <- colMeans(tau)   # P(X=t)

# Step 3. Covariate adjustment by residualizing the distal outcome

form_cov <- reformulate(covs, response = distal_var)

lm_cov <- lm(form_cov, data = analysis_df)

analysis_df$Ztilde <- resid(lm_cov)   # covariate adjusted residuals

# Vectors for ML step
Ztilde <- analysis_df$Ztilde
Wobs   <- analysis_df$class

# Step 4. Define the ML 3-step likelihood

logLik_ml3 <- function(par, Ztilde, Wobs, pi_hat, R) {
  T <- length(pi_hat)
  mu <- par[1:T]
  log_sigma <- par[T + 1]
  sigma <- exp(log_sigma)
  
  N <- length(Ztilde)
  ll <- 0
  
  for (i in 1:N) {
    s <- Wobs[i]  # observed assigned class (1..T)
    # density of Ztilde_i under each class t (this is the normal distribution assumption)
    dens_vec <- dnorm(Ztilde[i], mean = mu, sd = sigma, log = FALSE)
    # contribution for each class t
    comp <- pi_hat * dens_vec * R[, s]
    p_i <- sum(comp)
    if (!is.finite(p_i) || p_i <= .Machine$double.eps) {
      p_i <- .Machine$double.eps
    }
    ll <- ll + log(p_i)
  }
  
  return(-ll)   # negative log likelihood
}

# Step 5. Optimize the ML 3-step likelihood

mu0 <- tapply(analysis_df$Ztilde, analysis_df$class, mean)
log_sigma0 <- log(sd(analysis_df$Ztilde))

par0 <- c(mu0, log_sigma0)

fit_ml3 <- optim(
  par    = par0,
  fn     = logLik_ml3,
  Ztilde = Ztilde,
  Wobs   = Wobs,
  pi_hat = pi_hat,
  R      = R,
  method = "BFGS",
  hessian = TRUE
)

# fit_ml3$convergence  # should be 0 if successful

par_hat <- fit_ml3$par
Tcl <- length(pi_hat)

mu_hat      <- par_hat[1:Tcl]          # class specific adjusted means
log_sigma_h <- par_hat[Tcl + 1]
sigma_hat   <- exp(log_sigma_h)

vcov_par <- solve(fit_ml3$hessian)
se_par   <- sqrt(diag(vcov_par))

se_mu        <- se_par[1:Tcl]
se_log_sigma <- se_par[Tcl + 1]
se_sigma     <- sigma_hat * se_log_sigma

results_mu <- data.frame(
  class   = 1:Tcl,
  mu_hat  = mu_hat,
  se_mu   = se_mu,
  z_value = mu_hat / se_mu,
  p_value = 2 * pnorm(abs(mu_hat / se_mu), lower.tail = FALSE)
)
# results_mu


# Step 6. Optional: pairwise contrasts vs class 1

get_ml3_contrasts <- function(par_hat, vcov_par, ref = 1) {
  Tcl <- length(par_hat) - 1  # last parameter is log_sigma
  results <- list()
  
  for (t in seq_len(Tcl)) {
    if (t == ref) next
    
    # contrast vector: mu_t - mu_ref
    c_vec <- rep(0, length(par_hat))
    c_vec[t]   <- 1
    c_vec[ref] <- -1
    
    # point estimate
    est <- sum(c_vec * par_hat)
    
    # SE
    se  <- sqrt(t(c_vec) %*% vcov_par %*% c_vec)
    
    # z, p, CI
    z_val <- est / se
    p_val <- 2 * pnorm(abs(z_val), lower.tail = FALSE)
    ci_lo <- est - 1.96 * se
    ci_hi <- est + 1.96 * se
    
    results[[length(results) + 1]] <- data.frame(
      contrast = paste0("class", t, " - class", ref),
      estimate = est,
      std_error = se,
      z_value = z_val,
      p_value = p_val,
      CI_lower = ci_lo,
      CI_upper = ci_hi
    )
  }
  
  bind_rows(results)
}

contrasts_tbl <- get_ml3_contrasts(par_hat, vcov_par, ref = 1) 

# Get labeled class contrasts
contrasts_tbl$contrast <- contrasts_tbl$contrast %>%
    dplyr::recode(
      "class2 - class1" = "Slightly elevated BMI",
      "class3 - class1" = "High BMII",
      "class4 - class1" = "High BMI and Increasing"
    ) 

saveRDS(contrasts_tbl, file = here("Data", "Models", "model.LCA.ML.rds"))
```

```{r}
#| label: tbl-s8-lca-ml
#| tbl-cap: "Covariate-adjusted pairwise contrasts of log(hsCRP) between latent trajectory classes using the ML 3-step method, which accounts for classification uncertainty via the posterior probability matrix."
contrasts_tbl %>%
    select(-z_value) %>%
    gt() %>%
    tab_header(
      title = "Latent Class Contrasts for log(hsCRP)",
      subtitle = "Reference: Normal BMI Trajectory"
    ) %>%
    cols_label(
      contrast = "Comparison",
      estimate = "β",
      std_error = "SE",
      p_value = "p",
      CI_lower = "95% CI LL",
      CI_upper = "95% CI UL"
    ) %>%
    fmt_number(
      columns = c(estimate, std_error, CI_lower, CI_upper),
      decimals = 3
    ) %>%
    fmt(
      columns = p_value,
      fns = function(x) ifelse(x < 0.001, "<0.001", sprintf("%.3f", x))
    ) %>%
    tab_style(
      style = cell_text(weight = "bold"),
      locations = cells_body(
        columns = p_value,
        rows = p_value < 0.05
      )
    ) %>%
    tab_footnote(
      footnote = "Estimates adjusted for sex, age at hsCRP measurement, educational qualification, parental BMI, birthweight, persons per room, and father's occupation. Classification uncertainty accounted for using the ML 3-step approach."
    ) %>%
    tab_options(
      table.font.size = px(12),
      heading.title.font.size = px(16),
      heading.title.font.weight = "bold"
    )
```

<hr style="border: 2px solid black;">

# Distributed Lag Nonlinear Models (DLNM)

Distributed lag nonlinear models (DLNMs) represent life course exposure as a continuous function of both exposure intensity and timing by modeling an exposure-lag response surface using cross-basis functions.[@gasparrini2014; @zanobetti2000] The cumulative effects estimated by DLNMs can be interpreted as the expected difference in log(hsCRP) under sustained BMI levels (e.g., BMI 20 or BMI 30) relative to BMI 25 across adulthood.

All models were fit using the `dlnm` package in R.[@gasparrini2025] This section presents a series of DLNM specifications to evaluate sensitivity to modeling choices. Models were initially fit using BMI data across the full observed age range (ages 7--56), with a grid search over degrees of freedom (1--13) in both the exposure and lag dimensions to select the best-fitting unpenalized specification using AIC (@fig-s9-3d-unpenalized, @fig-s9-dlnm-full-unrestricted), and using penalized P-splines (@fig-s10-3d-penalized, @fig-s10-dlnm-full-penalized). The analysis is then repeated restricting to adult BMI only (ages 18--56) with Winsorized values to reduce the influence of extreme predictions, using both natural cubic splines (@fig-s11-3d-unpenalized, @fig-s11-dlnm-18-unrestricted) and penalized P-splines (@fig-s12-3d-penalized, @fig-s13-dlnm-18-penalized). Penalized models with higher and lower degrees of freedom were compared to assess overfitting and robustness; a final specification with lower degrees of freedom for the lag dimension provides a smoother and less over-fitted surface (@fig-s14-3d-lower, @fig-s15-dlnm-18-lower). Using the selected cross-basis specification, predictions were centered at BMI 25 and summarized as (i) lag-response patterns at fixed BMI values, (ii) exposure-response patterns at selected ages, and (iii) cumulative effects across the adult exposure window.

```{r}
library(dlnm)
library(plotly)
library(patchwork)
library(gratia)
library(mgcv)
set.seed(1234)

# Create a dataset for DLNM analysis
df.dlnm <- df.bmi_prediction %>%
  select(-sex) %>%
  right_join(df.model, by = "ncdsid")

# Reduce to 18+ to focus on adult BMI trajectories
df.dlnm.18 <- df.dlnm %>%
  filter(xage >= 18) 

# Windsorize predicted BMI values at the 1st and 99th percentiles to mitigate influence of extreme outliers in the DLNM analysis
quantiles <- quantile(df.dlnm.18$pred_bmi, c(0.01, 0.99), na.rm = TRUE)

df.dlnm.18.win <- df.dlnm.18 %>% 
  mutate(pred_bmi = pmax(pmin(pred_bmi, quantiles["99%"]), quantiles["1%"]))


```

```{r}
# Create a function for plotting DLNM Results
plot_dlnm <- function(pred_obj,
                        base_age = 7, # Define the base age corresponding to lag 0 (e.g., age 7 corresponds to lag 0, so lag 1 is age 8, etc.)
                        exposure_vals = c(20, 25, 30, 35), # Define specific exposure values (BMI) to plot in the lag-response curves
                        ages_to_show = c(18, 30, 40, 50), # Define specific ages to show in the exposure-response curves 
                        outcome_label = "log(hsCRP)", 
                        exposure_label = "BMI",
                        plot_type = "all") {

    # Setup
    lag_range <- 0:(ncol(pred_obj$matfit) - 1) # Define lag range based on the number of columns in matfit
    age_labels <- base_age + seq(0, max(lag_range), by = 10) # Define age labels for x-axis in lag-response plots
    available_vals <- pred_obj$predvar # Get available exposure values from the prediction object
    exposure_vals <- exposure_vals[exposure_vals %in% available_vals] # Filter exposure values to those available in the prediction object

    # 1. Data for lag-response (p1)
    df_slices <- map_dfr(exposure_vals, function(val) {
      row_idx <- which(pred_obj$predvar == val)
      tibble(
        lag = lag_range,
        age = lag + base_age,
        fit = pred_obj$matfit[row_idx, ],
        lci = pred_obj$matlow[row_idx, ],
        uci = pred_obj$mathigh[row_idx, ],
        exposure = factor(val, levels = exposure_vals)
      )
    })

    # 2. Data for exposure-response (p2)
    exp_range <- seq(min(exposure_vals), max(exposure_vals))
    exp_range <- exp_range[exp_range >= min(available_vals) & exp_range <= max(available_vals)]

    df_cross <- map_dfr(ages_to_show, function(age_val) {
      lag_val <- age_val - base_age
      if(lag_val >= 0 & lag_val <= max(lag_range)) {
        map_dfr(exp_range, function(val) {
          closest_idx <- which.min(abs(pred_obj$predvar - val))
          tibble(
            age = age_val,
            exposure = val,
            fit = pred_obj$matfit[closest_idx, lag_val + 1],
            lci = pred_obj$matlow[closest_idx, lag_val + 1],
            uci = pred_obj$mathigh[closest_idx, lag_val + 1]
          )
        })
      }
    })

    # 3. Data for cumulative (p3) - SEPARATE dataframe
    df_cumulative <- map_dfr(exposure_vals, function(val) {
      row_idx <- which(pred_obj$predvar == val)
      tibble(
        lag = lag_range,
        age = lag + base_age,
        fit = pred_obj$cumfit[row_idx, ],
        lci = pred_obj$cumlow[row_idx, ],
        uci = pred_obj$cumhigh[row_idx, ],
        exposure = factor(val, levels = exposure_vals)
      )
    })

    # Base theme
    theme_dlnm <- theme_minimal(base_size = 11) +
      theme(strip.text = element_text(size = 10, face = "bold"),
            legend.position = "none",
            panel.spacing = unit(1, "lines"),
            plot.margin = margin(5, 5, 5, 5),
            axis.title.y = element_text(margin = margin(r = 5)))

    # p1: Lag-response
    p1 <- ggplot(df_slices, aes(x = age, y = fit)) +
      geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "black", linewidth = 0.7) +
      geom_line(color = "#0072B2", linewidth = 1) +
      geom_ribbon(aes(ymin = lci, ymax = uci), fill = "gray70", alpha = 0.3) +
      facet_wrap(~ paste(exposure_label, "=", exposure), ncol = 1, scales = "free_y") +
      scale_x_continuous(breaks = age_labels, name = "Age") +
      labs(y = paste("Effect on", outcome_label), title = "Lag-response") +
      theme_dlnm

    # p2: Exposure-response
    p2 <- ggplot(df_cross, aes(x = exposure, y = fit)) +
      geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "black", linewidth = 0.7) +
      geom_line(color = "#009E73", linewidth = 1) +
      geom_ribbon(aes(ymin = lci, ymax = uci), fill = "gray70", alpha = 0.3) +
      facet_wrap(~ paste("Age =", age), ncol = 1, scales = "free_y") +
      scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(min(exp_range), max(exp_range), by = 5), name = exposure_label) +
      labs(y = paste("Effect on", outcome_label), title = "Exposure-response") +
      theme_dlnm

    # p3: Cumulative
    p3 <- ggplot(df_cumulative, aes(x = age, y = fit)) +
      geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "black", linewidth = 0.7) +
      geom_line(color = "#D55E00", linewidth = 1) +
      geom_ribbon(aes(ymin = lci, ymax = uci), fill = "gray80", alpha = 0.3) +
      facet_wrap(~ paste(exposure_label, "=", exposure), ncol = 1, scales = "free_y") +
      scale_x_continuous(breaks = age_labels, name = "Age") +
      labs(y = paste("Effect on", outcome_label), title = "Cumulative effects") +
      theme_dlnm
    # Return based on plot_type
    if (plot_type == "lag") return(p1)
    if (plot_type == "exposure") return(p2)
    if (plot_type == "cumulative") return(p3)
    if (plot_type == "all") return(p2 + p1 + p3 + plot_layout(axis_titles = "collect"))
  }
```

## Full Dataset

### Natural cubic spline in both dimensions with model selection by AIC

```{r}
#| eval: FALSE
# Define a grid of degrees of freedom to try
df.grid <- expand.grid(df.var = 3:13, df.lag = 3:13)

# Set up model storage
modellist <- list()
aiclist <- numeric(nrow(df.grid))

# Loop over grid combinations
for (i in seq_len(nrow(df.grid))) {
  dfv <- df.grid$df.var[i]
  dfl <- df.grid$df.lag[i]

  # Create crossbasis
  cb <- crossbasis(df.dlnm$pred_bmi, 
                   lag = 50, 
                   argvar = list(fun = "ns", df = dfv),
                   arglag = list(fun = "ns", df = dfl))

  # Fit model (no penalization here — pure lm)
  fit <- lm(loghsCRP ~ cb, data = df.dlnm)
  
  # Store model and AIC
  modellist[[i]] <- fit
  aiclist[i] <- AIC(fit)
  
  cat(sprintf("Model %d: df.var = %d, df.lag = %d --> AIC = %.2f\n", 
              i, dfv, dfl, aiclist[i]))
}

# Find best AIC
best <- which.min(aiclist)
best.grid <- df.grid[best, ]
best.model <- modellist[[best]]

cat("\n✔️ Best model using natural splines:\n")
print(best.grid)
```

```{r}
# Create the crossbasis with the best df combination from AIC selection
cb.bmi <- crossbasis(df.dlnm$pred_bmi, 
                     lag = 50, 
                     argvar = list(fun = "ns", df = 11),
                     arglag = list(fun = "ns", df = 13))

# Fit the DLNM model with the selected crossbasis and covariates
dlnm.fit <- lm(
  loghsCRP ~ cb.bmi + sex + sweep10_age + educ_qual + father_bmi + mother_bmi + n646 + n607 + n2383,
  data = df.dlnm
)

pred.cb.bmi <- crosspred(cb.bmi, dlnm.fit, at = 15:50, bylag = 1, cen = 25, cumul = TRUE)

# Define the base age corresponding to lag 0 (e.g., if lag 0 corresponds to age 7, then lag 1 is age 8, etc.)
base_age <- 7

```

```{r}
#| label: fig-s9-3d-unpenalized
#| fig-cap: "Three-dimensional exposure-lag-response surface for the association between BMI and log(hsCRP) across ages 7--56, modeled with AIC-selected natural cubic splines (df.var = 11, df.lag = 13), centered at BMI = 25."
#| fig-height: 5
#| fig-width: 5

# Create a grid for the surface plot
x <- pred.cb.bmi$predvar           # BMI (exposure)
y <- (0:(ncol(pred.cb.bmi$matfit)-1)) + base_age  # age at exposure
# Transpose matfit to match dimensions: rows = lag, columns = BMI
z <- t(pred.cb.bmi$matfit)  # Now: 51 rows (lags) × 41 columns (BMI)

# Create 3D surface plot
plot_ly(
  x = ~x,
  y = ~y,
  z = ~z,
  type = "surface",
  colorscale = "Viridis",
  showscale = FALSE
) %>%
  layout(
    scene = list(
      xaxis = list(title = "BMI", showgrid = TRUE, gridwidth = 1),
      yaxis = list(title = "Age at Exposure", showgrid = TRUE, gridwidth = 1),
      zaxis = list(title = "Effect on log(hsCRP)", showgrid = TRUE, gridwidth = 1),
      camera = list(
        eye = list(x = 1.5, y = 1.5, z = 1.5)
      )
    ),
    title = "Exposure–Lag–Response Surface"
  )

```

```{r}
#| label: fig-s9-dlnm-full-unrestricted
#| fig-cap: "DLNM results for the full age range (7--56) with natural cubic splines: exposure-response curves at selected ages, lag-response curves at selected BMI values, and cumulative effects over the life course."
#| fig-height: 7

# Call the plotting function with the prediction object from the best model
plot_dlnm(pred.cb.bmi,
            base_age = base_age,
            exposure_vals = c(20, 25, 30, 35),
            ages_to_show = c(18, 30, 40, 50),
            outcome_label = "log(hsCRP)",
            exposure_label = "BMI")
```

### Penalized DLNM with P-splines in both dimensions

```{r}
#| eval: FALSE
library(mgcv)
# Define DF values to try
df.grid.pen <- expand.grid(df.var = 4:13, df.lag = 4:13)

# Create list to store models
modellist <- list()
edflist <- numeric(nrow(df.grid.pen))

# Loop through each df combination
for (i in seq_len(nrow(df.grid.pen))) {
  dfv <- df.grid.pen$df.var[i]
  dfl <- df.grid.pen$df.lag[i]
  
  # Define crossbasis
  cb <- crossbasis(df.dlnm$pred_bmi, 
                   lag = 50,
                   argvar = list(fun = "ps", df = dfv),
                   arglag = list(fun = "ps", df = dfl))
  
  # Get penalty structure
  cb.pen <- cbPen(cb)
  
  # Fit penalized GAM
  fit <- gam(loghsCRP ~ cb, 
             paraPen = list(cb = cb.pen), 
             method = "GCV.Cp", 
             data = df.dlnm)
  
  modellist[[i]] <- fit
  edflist[i] <- sum(fit$edf[-1])
  
  cat(sprintf("Done: df.var = %d, df.lag = %d → edf = %.2f → total df = %.2f\n", dfv, dfl, edflist[i],dfv*dfl ))
}

# Find best model
penaltyproportion <- (edflist / (df.grid.pen$df.var * df.grid.pen$df.lag))
# gcv_vals <- sapply(modellist, function(x) x$gcv.ubre)
data.frame(df.grid.pen,edflist,penaltyproportion )  %>% 
  mutate(totaldf = df.var * df.lag) %>% 
  relocate(penaltyproportion, .after=totaldf)

# best <- which.min(edflist)
# best.grid <- df.grid.pen[best, ]
# best.model <- modellist[[best]]

# cat("\n✔️ Best model found:\n")
# print(best.grid)
```

```{r}
# Create the crossbasis with the best df combination from AIC selection
cb.bmi <- crossbasis(df.dlnm$pred_bmi, 
                         lag = 50,
                         argvar = list(fun = "ps", df = 11),
                         arglag = list(fun = "ps", df = 13))

# Define the penalty for the crossbasis
cb.bmi.pen <- cbPen(cb.bmi)

dlnm.gam <- gam(
  loghsCRP ~ cb.bmi + sex + sweep10_age + educ_qual + father_bmi + mother_bmi + n646 + n607 + n2383,
  paraPen = list(cb.bmi = cb.bmi.pen),
  method = "GCV.Cp",
  data = df.dlnm
)

pred.cb.bmi.pen <- crosspred(cb.bmi, dlnm.gam, at = 15:50, bylag = 1, cen = 25, cumul = TRUE)

# Define the base age corresponding to lag 0 (e.g., if lag 0 corresponds to age 7, then lag 1 is age 8, etc.)
base_age <- 7

```

```{r}
#| label: fig-s10-3d-penalized
#| fig-cap: "Three-dimensional exposure-lag-response surface for the full age range (7--56) using penalized P-splines with GCV-based smoothness selection, centered at BMI = 25."
#| fig-height: 5
#| fig-width: 5
x <- pred.cb.bmi.pen$predvar           # BMI (exposure)
y <- (0:(ncol(pred.cb.bmi.pen$matfit)-1)) + base_age  # age at exposure
# Transpose matfit to match dimensions: rows = lag, columns = BMI
z <- t(pred.cb.bmi.pen$matfit)  # Now: 51 rows (lags) × 41 columns (BMI)

# Create 3D surface plot
plot_ly(
  x = ~x,
  y = ~y,
  z = ~z,
  type = "surface",
  colorscale = "Viridis",
  showscale = FALSE
) %>%
  layout(
    scene = list(
      xaxis = list(title = "BMI", showgrid = TRUE, gridwidth = 1),
      yaxis = list(title = "Age at Exposure", showgrid = TRUE, gridwidth = 1),
      zaxis = list(title = "Effect on log(hsCRP)", showgrid = TRUE, gridwidth = 1),
      camera = list(
        eye = list(x = 1.5, y = 1.5, z = 1.5)
      )
    ),
    title = "Exposure–Lag–Response Surface"
  )

```

```{r}
#| label: fig-s10-dlnm-full-penalized
#| fig-cap: "DLNM results for the full age range (7--56) with penalized P-splines: exposure-response curves at selected ages, lag-response curves at selected BMI values, and cumulative effects."
#| fig-height: 7

plot_dlnm(pred.cb.bmi.pen,
            base_age = 7,
            exposure_vals = c(20, 25, 30, 35),
            ages_to_show = c(18, 30, 40, 50),
            outcome_label = "log(hsCRP)",
            exposure_label = "BMI")
```

## Reduce to Adult BMI Only (18+) and Windsorize

### Natural cubic spline in both dimensions with model selection by AIC

```{r}
#| eval: FALSE
df.grid <- expand.grid(df.var = 3:13, df.lag = 3:13)

# Set up model storage
modellist <- list()
aiclist <- numeric(nrow(df.grid))

# Loop over grid combinations
for (i in seq_len(nrow(df.grid))) {
  dfv <- df.grid$df.var[i]
  dfl <- df.grid$df.lag[i]

  # Create crossbasis
  cb <- crossbasis(df.dlnm.18.win$pred_bmi, 
                   lag = 39, 
                   argvar = list(fun = "ns", df = dfv),
                   arglag = list(fun = "ns", df = dfl))

  # Fit model (no penalization here — pure lm)
  fit <- lm(loghsCRP ~ cb, data = df.dlnm.18.win)
  
  # Store model and AIC
  modellist[[i]] <- fit
  aiclist[i] <- AIC(fit)
  
  cat(sprintf("Model %d: df.var = %d, df.lag = %d --> AIC = %.2f\n", 
              i, dfv, dfl, aiclist[i]))
}

# Find best AIC
best <- which.min(aiclist)
best.grid <- df.grid[best, ]
best.model <- modellist[[best]]

cat("\n✔️ Best model using natural splines:\n")
print(best.grid)
```

```{r}

cb.bmi.18 <- crossbasis(df.dlnm.18.win$pred_bmi, 
                     lag = 39, 
                     argvar = list(fun = "ns", df = 6),
                     arglag = list(fun = "ns", df = 13))

dlnm.fit <- lm(
```
